content.text: Mount vs Update: The Key Differences

The mounting and updating phases share the same two-step structure — render then commit — but differ in their triggers and in what the commit step does. Understanding the differences explains why certain patterns work on mount but break on update.

attrs.alt: Mount vs update phases: side-by-side step comparison

content.text: The Mount Render Phase
During mount, React calls the component function for the first time. There is no previous virtual DOM to compare against — React creates the entire fiber tree from scratch.
Passing a function to
useState

(lazy initialization) is the correct way to avoid repeating expensive computations that should only happen at initialization time. The function runs exactly once on mount and is ignored on every subsequent render.
The Update Render Phase: Reconciliation

During an update, React calls the component function again with the new props and state, then compares the new virtual DOM tree against the previous one in a process called reconciliation. Only the nodes that differ produce DOM mutations.
React's reconciliation uses the component type and
key
prop to match elements in the new tree against the previous tree. Same type + same key → update the existing DOM node. Different type or no key match → unmount old, mount new.
Effects on Mount: All Effects Run
On mount, every
useEffect
and
useLayoutEffect
setup function runs, regardless of its dependency array. The dependency array only prevents re-runs during updates — it has no effect on whether a hook runs on mount.
Common misconception:
Developers sometimes think that
useEffect(fn, [])
means the effect "runs only once ever." More precisely, it runs once per mount. If the component unmounts and remounts (e.g., its key changes), the effect runs again.
Effects on Update: Only Changed Deps Re-Run

On update, only effects whose dependency arrays contain a changed value re-run. For each re-running effect, React first calls the cleanup function from the previous run, then calls the setup function again.
Batching: Multiple State Updates in One Render
In React 18+, all state updates from the same event handler, async function, or timeout are batched into a single re-render.
Rendering Bailout Mechanisms
React can bail out of an update without re-rendering if it determines nothing has changed. Hooks like
useState
and
useReducer
compare the new value with the previous one using
Object.is
. If they are equal, React skips the re-render.

Bailout mechanisms are optimisations, not guarantees. React may choose to re-render even when props appear unchanged (e.g., after a concurrent mode interruption). Never rely on render counting for business logic.
Internal Representation: Fibers

React tracks components through an internal data structure called a fiber. Each component instance has a fiber that stores its hooks, state, and a reference to its DOM node. On mount, new fibers are created. On update, existing fibers are mutated in place.

React maintains two fiber trees: current (on screen) and work-in-progress (being built). When a render completes, the work-in-progress tree becomes current. This double-buffering ensures the UI is always in a consistent state.
What Triggers an Update
State changes:
setState
or
dispatch
called with a value not equal to the previous one (
Object.is
comparison).
Props change:
Parent re-renders and passes different props. Without
React.memo
, a child re-renders whenever its parent does, even if the props object has the same values.
Context changes:
A
Context.Provider
value changes — all consumers re-render.
Parent re-render:
The most common source of unexpected re-renders. Every child in a rendering parent's JSX re-renders by default.
Detecting Unnecessary Re-Renders
React DevTools Profiler records which components rendered and why. To find unnecessary re-renders:
Open DevTools → Profiler tab → Record.
Interact with your app (click a button, type in an input).
Stop recording and inspect the flame graph.
Components highlighted in the profiler that you expect not to have changed are candidates for
React.memo
The "Why did this render?" tooltip shows which props or state changed — verify it is intentional.
Summary

Mount creates the fiber tree from scratch and runs all effects for the first time. Update diffs the new virtual DOM against the previous one, applies minimal DOM patches, and re-runs only the effects whose dependencies changed (cleanup before setup). React 18 batches all state updates. Bailout mechanisms let React skip renders and effect re-runs when values have not changed.
Derived State During Render
React does not have a hook equivalent of
getDerivedStateFromProps
. Instead, derive values directly in the component body — this is called computing "derived state during render" and is idiomatic modern React.
Deriving values during render avoids an extra render cycle and is always up-to-date. Use
useMemo
only when the computation is genuinely expensive — simple array operations and string formatting do not need it.
Tracking Previous Values
If you need the previous value of a prop or state inside an effect, a
useRef
that updates after each render is the canonical pattern.

The ref is updated inside the effect (after the render cycle) so it always holds the value from the previous render. Updating it during render would give you the current value, which defeats the purpose.
flushSync: Opting Out of Batching
By default, state updates inside event handlers are batched. If you need a synchronous update — for example, to read a DOM layout immediately after setting state — you can opt out of batching with
flushSync
Use sparingly:
flushSync
runs synchronous renders that may include multiple re-renders and block the browser. Reserve it for cases where you must read the DOM immediately after a state update.
Concurrent Features: Transitions
React 18 introduced
startTransition

to mark state updates as non-urgent. Transition updates can be interrupted by more urgent ones (like typing), and React defers them until the main thread is idle. This keeps the UI responsive while expensive updates (like filtering a large list) process in the background.

Transitions do not delay the update infinitely — they simply allow React to prioritise more urgent updates (the input value) over less urgent ones (the filtered list). If nothing more urgent arrives, the transition renders immediately.
Error Boundaries and the Update Phase
If a component throws during render or during a lifecycle method (the update phase), React catches the error at the nearest error boundary and renders its fallback UI instead of the crashed subtree.

Function components cannot be error boundaries yet — you need a class component. Errors thrown during event handlers are NOT caught by error boundaries — only errors during render, useEffect, and useLayoutEffect are caught.

Practice: in a component that fetches data and subscribes to a channel, trace the exact sequence of renders, effect setups, and cleanups for (1) initial mount, (2) a prop change that affects only one effect, and (3) a state change that does not affect either effect.
